items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.q: What is the principle behind stellar parallax?
items.correct: A nearby star appears to shift against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: Stars actually move position throughout the year.
items.why: Students think stars literally move. Parallax is an APPARENT shift due to our changing viewpoint - same reason your finger seems to jump when you alternate eyes. The closer the star, the bigger the shift.

items.q: What is the point directly above an observer called?
items.why: It's Zenith. “Nadir” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the Sun's annual apparent path called?
items.why: It's Ecliptic. “Celestial equator” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: By approximately how many degrees is the ecliptic tilted relative to the celestial equator?
items.why: The real figure is 23.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
